stages.label: Stage 1
stages.description: Date Wednesday 17 June Start Sondrio Finish Sondrio Distance 144.0 km Elevation gain 2455 m Start time 14:15 Expected finish 17:35-17:55 Stage 1 Sondrio - Sondrio (144.0km) The opening stage of the 2026 Tour de Suisse starts and finishes in the Italian town of Sondrio and promises immediate excitement. The route is technical and hilly, featuring several short but steep climbs that offer early opportunities for attackers and puncheurs. The general classification riders will also need to stay alert from day one to avoid losing time in the tricky finale. With 2,455 metres of elevation gain over a distance of 144 kilometres, it will be a demanding opening stage right away.

stages.label: Stage 2
stages.description: Date Thursday 18 June Start Locarno Finish Locarno Distance 157.7 km Elevation gain 2110 m Start time 14:00 Expected finish 17:35-17:55 Stage 2 Locarno - Locarno (157.7km) The second stage of the 2026 Tour de Suisse travels through the canton of Ticino and combines a rolling route with a tricky finale. Along the way, the riders face several short climbs, giving attackers and strong classics specialists the chance to make their move. The final kilometres still feature a few punchy ascents, making a sprint from a reduced peloton a likely outcome. With 2,110 metres of elevation gain over 157.7 kilometres, another demanding and selective stage awaits.

stages.label: Stage 3
stages.description: Date Friday 19 June Start Bad Ragaz Finish Bad Ragaz Distance 157.4 km Elevation gain 2690 m Start time 13:45 Expected finish 17:34-17:57 Stage 3 Bad Ragaz - Bad Ragaz (157.4km) The third stage of the 2026 Tour de Suisse is a hilly ride around Bad Ragaz that looks ideal for attackers and strong climbers. In the first part of the stage, the climbs come one after another, with several longer ascents capable of putting the peloton under serious pressure. After a selective middle section, the route becomes slightly easier towards the finale, but the repeated efforts mean that only the strongest riders are likely to contest the victory. With 2,690 metres of elevation gain over 157.9 kilometres, this will once again be a tough day in Switzerland.

stages.label: Stage 4
stages.description: Date Saturday 20 June Start Aarburg Finish Aarburg Distance 23.8 km Elevation gain 270 m Start time 15:04 Expected finish 17:30 Type Individual Time Trial Stage 4 Aarburg - Aarburg (23.8km) The fourth stage of the 2026 Tour de Suisse is an individual time trial of 23.8 kilometres in and around Aarburg. On paper, the course looks fairly fast, but it includes several rolling sections and short climbs that could disrupt the riders’ rhythm. Time trial specialists will have an excellent opportunity to gain time, while the general classification contenders must also perform strongly to avoid losing valuable seconds. With only 270 metres of elevation gain, this stage appears to favour pure power and aerodynamics.

stages.label: Stage 5
stages.description: Date Sunday 21 June Start Villars-sur-Ollon Finish Villars-sur-Ollon Distance 150.7 km Elevation gain 4226 m Start time 13:30 Expected finish 17:18-17:42 Stage 5 Villars-sur-Ollon - Villars-sur-Ollon (150.7km) The fifth and final stage of the 2026 Tour de Suisse will also be the queen stage of this edition. Starting from Villars-sur-Ollon, the peloton faces a brutal mountain stage featuring no less than 4,226 metres of elevation gain over 151.1 kilometres. The route consists of a succession of long climbs and technical descents, meaning the general classification could be completely reshaped here. The final climb to Villars-sur-Ollon will provide the ultimate battleground for the climbers and GC contenders, who will give everything on the final day to secure overall victory.

stages.label: Stage 1
stages.description: During the start of the Tour, ASO is introducing something new. As has happened several times throughout history, the race will begin with a team time trial. But this time, it is not the time of the fifth finisher that counts—the time will be recorded individually for each rider. This makes the 19-kilometer time trial through Barcelona immediately interesting. In the final phase, the route becomes hectic and challenging as the riders climb Montjuïc from Plaza de España (1.1 kilometers at 5.1%). Once at the top, a final stretch of 800 meters at an average gradient of around 7 percent awaits. During the start of the Tour, ASO is introducing something new. As has happened several times throughout history, the race will begin with a team time trial. But this time, it is not the time of the fifth finisher that counts—the time will be recorded individually for each rider. This makes the 19-kilometer time trial through Barcelona immediately interesting.

stages.label: Stage 2
stages.description: The stage begins on Sunday in the ancient Roman port city of Tarragona, after which the riders cover the first 90 kilometers along the coast before heading into the Catalan interior. There the climbs begin, starting halfway through the stage with the Côte de Begues (6.1 kilometers at 6.5%). After this climb, there is a brief lull, but with just over fifty kilometers to go, the race—at least in terms of climbing—bursts open. First comes the longer ascent of the Côte de Santa Creu d’Olorda (8.4 kilometers at 4.5%), followed by real leg-breakers on a local circuit in Barcelona that will cause plenty of pain. On that local circuit, the Côte du Château de Montjuïc (1.3 kilometers at 9.3%) and the Côte du Stade Olympique (600 meters at 7%) must be conquered three times, and it is very likely that the first of these climbs, with its steep gradients, will create casualties. Especially since the summit of that climb comes only 2.3 kilometers before the finish line.

stages.label: Stage 3
stages.description: Monday’s third stage still starts in Granollers, a small town north of Barcelona. Over gently rolling roads, the peloton heads toward the Pyrenees. Early in the stage, the Sant Feliu de Codines (7.6 kilometers at 4.5%) must be climbed. The ascent is not too difficult, but it is an ideal moment for an early breakaway to gain a solid lead over the peloton. Only in the second half of the stage does the climbing resume. After nearly 120 kilometers, the riders begin the toughest climb of the day on paper: the Collada de Toses (9.3 kilometers at 6.5%). After a long descent, the riders reach France for the final part of the stage. Awaiting the peloton is the Col du Calvaire, an uncategorized but long climb of 14.9 kilometers. With gradients around 3 to 4 percent, this climb should not cause major damage. In the final phase of the stage, the riders climb toward Lac de Matemale, where the final ascent to Les Angles (1.7 kilometers at 7.6%) begins. In these uphill kilometers, explosive riders may still be able to force something.

stages.label: Stage 4
stages.description: Stage four is a true transitional stage, the kind you would normally see only in the second or third week of the Tour de France. It is far too difficult for sprinters, while GC contenders will certainly not make their move here yet. So it will likely be a day for the breakaway. In the first hours of racing, there will be few opportunities to really get away. Yes, the Col de Villerouge and the Col de Bedos are on the route, but these climbs never really get steeper than four or five percent. The same goes for the Col du Paradis after about sixty kilometers. After halfway through the race, the real climbs and mountain points follow. After nearly 100 kilometers, the riders begin the ascent of the Col de Coudons (10.7 kilometers at 5.5%), where we will undoubtedly see a strong battle among the breakaway riders. After this climb, the riders remain on a plateau for quite some time before descending for about thirty kilometers toward Bélesta.

stages.label: Stage 5
stages.description: The stage is only 158 kilometers long and makes a northern loop around the Pyrenees, largely through the Gers Valley. When the riders reach Vic-en-Bigorre, there are still 45 kilometers left to the finish in Pau. Here, three small climbs follow within twelve kilometers: the Côte de Casteide-Doat (1.5 kilometers at 5.1%), Côte de Flancart (1 kilometer at 6.4%), and Côte de Baleix (1.3 kilometers at 7%). After that last climb, 26 flat kilometers remain to the finish in Pau, where a Tour stage will end for the 64th time in history. The last time the Tour finished in Pau was in 2024, when Jasper Philipsen took the victory. After four days of racing through the Catalan hills and the Pyrenees, the sprinters will finally get their first mass sprint on day five—at least, that is the expectation. The stage takes the riders from Lannemezan to Pau.

stages.label: Stage 6
stages.description: After the start in Pau, a calm and classic approach toward the Pyrenees awaits. Via Lourdes and Bagnères-de-Bigorre, the riders continue to Arreau, where the foot of the first Pyrenean giant of the day lies: the Col d’Aspin (12 kilometers at 6.5%). On the profile chart, the Aspin may look like a speed bump compared to what follows, but the climb is truly tough—especially because its steepest kilometers consistently rise above 9 percent. After the Aspin, the race descends to Sainte-Marie-de-Campan, where the ascent of the biggest mountain of the day begins: the mythical Col du Tourmalet (17 kilometers at 7.3%). The longer the Tourmalet goes on, the steeper it becomes. From the tenth kilometer onward, the gradient barely drops below nine percent. At 2,115 meters, a mountain sprint for the Souvenir Jacques Goddet awaits, after which 39 kilometers remain to the finish. The final 39 kilometers of the stage are not overly difficult, although… After a 22-kilometer descent, one more climb of 18 kilometers with an average gradient of 3.7 percent remains. At this point, it would be very useful for a GC rider to still have a teammate by their side.

stages.label: Stage 7
stages.description: This Friday’s stage starts in the little-known town of Hagetmau. This small village of just 5,000 inhabitants has never before hosted the Tour de France, so it will certainly be a big celebration. After the start, the riders will quickly head north toward Bordeaux. There won’t be much excitement along the way, as the roads are mostly flat. On the way to the sprinter city of Bordeaux, the only climb is the Côte de Béguey (1.6 kilometers at 4.3%), located 40 kilometers from the finish, but no climber is expected to be dropped here. So a ‘regular’ sprint is expected in Bordeaux. The capital of the Gironde has already hosted a Tour stage 133 times, and in the past it has predominantly been sprinters who won here. Think of Jasper Philipsen in 2023 or Mark Cavendish in 2010. In the past, Dutch riders have also won here regularly. Notable examples include Cees Priem, Bert Oosterbosch, Jan Raas, and Jean-Paul van Poppel in the 1980s, and of course Servais Knaven in 2003. He is the last Dutchman to win here.

stages.label: Stage 8
stages.description: Saturday’s route is slightly more varied than Friday’s. In the first eighty kilometers, the riders head east toward Vézac, before continuing through the Dordogne Valley toward Bergerac. The sprinters who messed up their sprint in Bordeaux on Friday will immediately have a chance to redeem themselves on day eight of the Tour de France. On Saturday, the riders face an almost entirely flat 182-kilometer stage from Périgueux to Bergerac. Saturday’s route is slightly more varied than Friday’s. In the first eighty kilometers, the riders head east toward Vézac, before continuing through the Dordogne Valley toward Bergerac.

stages.label: Stage 9
stages.description: The start takes place Sunday in Malemort. Late in the morning, the riders set off on a 185-kilometer stage. The terrain rolls up and down all day as the riders wind their way north, with uncategorized climbs such as the Puy Boubou (2.8 kilometers at 4.1%), Côte de Lagleygeolle (5.2 kilometers at 3.9%), and Côte de Miel (6.6 kilometers at 3.9%). Midway through the stage, the Côte de Naves (2.8 kilometers at 6.7%) and the Puy de Lachaud (3.6 kilometers at 5.3%) follow. After 105 kilometers, the Suc au May (3.9 kilometers at 7.7%) is the only categorized climb of the day. In the final part of the race, several tough climbs still await. Consider the Mont Bessou (800 meters at 8.5%), located 24 kilometers from the finish, climbing at around eight percent for a full kilometer. The final hill is the Côte des Gardes (2.2 kilometers at 4.8%), 14 kilometers from the finish line.

stages.label: Stage 10
stages.description: In this tenth stage, the peloton travels 167 kilometers through the Massif Central. The riders start in the afternoon in Aurillac, followed by a flat opening phase. Only in the final eighty kilometers does the vast majority of the 3,900 meters of climbing appear. After nearly 100 kilometers, the riders reach the top of the first official climb of the day: the Col de la Griffoul (5.9 kilometers at 6.7%). From here on, the stage contains no flat sections at all. There are the ascents of the Col de Prat de Bouc and the Côte de Murat, but no mountain points are awarded at their summits. After nearly 100 kilometers, the riders crest the first official climb, the Col de la Griffoul—5.9 kilometers at 6.7%. Soon after comes the Col de Prat de Bouc (3.2 kilometers at 5.8%), and directly from the descent, the Côte de Murat (6.6 kilometers at 4.4%). Strictly speaking, these are nothing more than preliminary skirmishes.

stages.label: Stage 11
stages.description: There is little excitement along the way on Wednesday. After leaving Vichy, the riders follow the Allier River for a long stretch. Near Moulins, they then head toward the Loire Valley. Once they have crossed it, they take a detour into Nevers. A sprint will then decide the stage. This will be the fourth time the Tour has finished in Nevers: in the past, winners here have included Eric Leman, Guido Bontempo, and Alessandro Petacchi. The sprinters get another chance at stage victory in the second week. The 161 kilometers between Vichy and Nevers are almost entirely flat, so few riders will entertain the illusion of preventing a bunch sprint.

stages.label: Stage 12
stages.description: From the circuit, the riders head east on Thursday. As in previous stages, they cross the Loire before eventually reaching the Morvan Nature Park. Here awaits the only obstacle of the day: the Côte de Montagny-lès-Buxy, a climb of just 2.6 kilometers with an average gradient of 3.9 percent. Will climbers really be dropped here? We can hardly imagine it. Therefore, the peloton will spend the final twenty kilometers preparing for a mass sprint. Thursday is another day for the sprinters! After twelve days of racing, it will already be the fifth opportunity for a bunch sprint in the Tour de France. Between the Circuit de Nevers Magny-Cours and Chalon-sur-Saône, there are hardly any elevation changes.

stages.label: Stage 13
stages.description: The thirteenth stage covers a total of 205 kilometers, making it the longest stage in this Tour de France. It is also the only stage longer than 200 kilometers. Before any real climbing begins, the riders must cover roughly 150 kilometers northeast from the start in Dole. The first climb comes only after 150 kilometers, over the Col des Croix (5.4 kilometers at 4.9%). No major casualties are expected here, but the next climb will likely make a difference. The Tour revisits the Ballon d’Alsace (8.7 kilometers at 6.9%), which, 121 years ago, was the first real climb in Tour history—a moment that will surely be commemorated this Friday. The Ballon d’Alsace is a steady climb, but it still has a peak section of 8.8 percent along the way.

stages.label: Stage 14
stages.description: From Mulhouse, the riders quickly reach the foot of the first climb of the day: the Grand Ballon (21.5 kilometers at 4.8%). Although the climb can be approached from seven different sides, it is actually tougher than it looks on paper. This is because there are some flat and descending sections within the climb. Remove those, and about fifteen kilometers remain at gradients of 7 to 8 percent. After the Grand Ballon, the riders descend to the first passage of the finish in Le Markstein, followed by a middle section including the Col du Page (9.8 kilometers at 4.7%) and once again the Ballon d’Alsace (9.8 kilometers at 6.9%). After these climbs, the stage awaits its final ascent. The Col du Schirm and Col du Hundsruck must still be climbed, but they pale in comparison to what comes at the finish.

stages.label: Stage 15
stages.description: The stage starts on Sunday in Champagnole, in the Jura. From this picturesque village—surrounded by plenty of greenery along the Ain—the riders begin climbing almost immediately. They tackle the Mont Rivel right away, a climb 22 kilometers long but with an average gradient of only 2.1 percent. After this ascent comes a short descent leading to the Côte de Doppes (15.2 kilometers at 3.6%). This climb is also very long but again has a relatively low gradient. Next follows a descent of roughly 50 kilometers with some short climbs, until about 60 kilometers from the finish. Here, the first truly serious climb appears: the Col de la Croissette (7.6 kilometers at 8.8%), after which approximately fifty kilometers remain to the finish. In the finale, the Plateau de Solaison (11.3 kilometers at 9.1%) awaits as a beast of a final climb. This ascent, which was also featured a few years ago in the Dauphiné, hits hard immediately with gradients over ten percent. After that, the gradient oscillates between 8 and 10 percent all the way to the summit.

stages.label: Stage 16
stages.description: The time trial takes the riders along the lake from Évian-les-Bains to Thonon-les-Bains and includes approximately 500 meters of elevation gain. This is due to a hill immediately after the start: a climb 9.6 kilometers long with an average gradient of 4.2 percent. After seven kilometers, the course descends back down to the lake. From the lake, nine kilometers remain to the finish in Thonon-les-Bains. This time trial could once again shake up the general classification significantly. After the Jura, the Tour riders head quickly to the Alps, where the big showdown of the Tour de France is set to take place. On the Tuesday following the second rest day, a 26-kilometer time trial awaits at Lake Geneva, specifically in Thonon-les-Bains.

stages.label: Stage 17
stages.description: After a flat opening from Chambéry, the riders climb for about thirty kilometers after roughly twenty kilometers of racing. Nowhere is it truly steep, as the highest point of the stage is only around 1,100 meters: the Col des Prés. Only the final kilometers are somewhat challenging, with a 3.5-kilometer stretch averaging 6.6 percent. This is followed by a descent back to Chambéry and the Col de Couz (8.6 kilometers at 2.8%). This hill is the last real obstacle before the finish, which is still 85 kilometers away. Nowhere in the last two hours is it entirely flat, but it is far from difficult. The final 35 kilometers even mostly descend. In the finale, a small hill of 2.5 kilometers at 3.5 percent awaits—but will it be enough to prevent a sprint in Voiron? So late in the Tour de France, you never really know.

stages.label: Stage 18
stages.description: After the start, the riders head south toward Grenoble, where the first climb of the day awaits: the Engins (11.4 kilometers at 5.4%). After a long plateau section, the peloton briefly enters the valley. Midway through the stage, the climb to Monteynard (9.7 kilometers at 5.2%) follows. After this climb, the riders encounter a long plateau. Only 32 kilometers from the finish does the climbing resume, though still at gentle gradients. The real challenge begins just seven kilometers from the finish with the ascent to Orcières-Merlette (7.1 kilometers at 6.8%). The Alps had been looming on the horizon in the Tour de France, but on Thursday, it is finally time: the first true Alpine stage, with a total of 3,800 meters of climbing. The stage starts in the morning from Voiron, covering 185 kilometers to the ski resort of Orcières-Merlette.

stages.label: Stage 19
stages.description: The start, as has often been the case in Tour history, is in Gap. From Gap, the riders face a 128-kilometer stage with a total of four climbs. The first comes immediately after the start: the Col de Bayard (5.1 kilometers at 7.2%). Directly after the descent, the Col du Noyer (7.2 kilometers at 8.5%) follows. The peloton will already be considerably thinned out after this tough opening phase—only 25 kilometers have been covered! After these two climbs, there is a long period of relative calm. Only 75 kilometers later does climbing resume with the Col d’Ornon (5.4 kilometers at 6.4%), signaling the approach to the finale. After a 13-kilometer descent, the riders arrive in Bourg d’Oisans. From there, you know what’s coming: Alpe d’Huez (13.8 kilometers at 8.1%) begins!

stages.label: Stage 20
stages.description: The stage starts on this Saturday in Bourg d’Oisans. This town lies at the foot of both Alpe d’Huez and the Col de la Croix de Fer (24 kilometers at 5.2%). The riders tackle the Croix de Fer first, reaching an altitude of over 2,000 meters after just 34 kilometers of racing. After the summit of the Croix de Fer, the riders descend toward Saint-Jean-de-Maurienne, signaling the upcoming combination of Col du Télégraphe and Col du Galibier. The Télégraphe (11.9 kilometers at 7.1%) may seem like a minor obstacle compared to the other climbs in this stage, but it will greatly increase fatigue among the riders. Especially since there is no descent after this climb—it almost immediately continues into the Col du Galibier (17.7 kilometers at 6.9%). At 2,642 meters, this is instantly the highest point of the Tour. After the Galibier, there are still 61 kilometers remaining in the stage.

stages.label: Stage 21
stages.description: The approach to Paris will be leisurely, with the riders covering roughly fifty kilometers from Thoiry while chatting, taking photos, and joking with one another. With just under 80 kilometers remaining, the peloton enters Paris, and the racing truly begins. The stage starts with three laps of 6.8 kilometers around and along the Champs-Élysées, followed by three more laps of a 16.7-kilometer finishing circuit. Each of these laps features the Côte de la Butte Montmartre (1.1 kilometers at 5.9%), with the final ascent coming 6.1 kilometers from the finish. Whether there will be a sprint is still uncertain. In 2025—when Montmartre was first included in the Tour—the climb provided plenty of excitement. On a soaking wet course, five riders broke away from a leading group in the finale, with Wout van Aert managing to drop Tadej Pogačar. The Slovenian, however, secured the yellow jersey soon after.

stages.label: Stage 16
stages.finish_town: La Rábida - Palos de la Frontera

stages.label: Stage 1
stages.description: The Baloise Belgium Tour opens with a rolling 187.9 km stage around Scherpenheuvel-Zichem. With 1,431 metres of elevation gain and numerous short climbs, it will be far from an easy opening day. A reduced bunch sprint appears to be the most likely outcome, although attackers will certainly have opportunities along the way.

stages.label: Stage 2
stages.description: The second stage runs from Merelbeke-Melle to Knokke-Heist over 197.6 kilometres. With only 831 metres of elevation gain, the route is considerably flatter than the opening stage, making it an attractive prospect for the sprinters. However, coastal winds could create nervous racing. The slightly uphill finishing straight in Knokke-Heist will make timing crucial and could favour the more explosive sprinters.

stages.label: Stage 3
stages.description: The queen stage of this Baloise Belgium Tour takes place in the rolling terrain of Wallonia. Over 173 kilometres, the riders face 2,974 metres of elevation gain and a relentless succession of short, steep climbs. The local laps around Durbuy add another layer of difficulty, making the course highly selective. This is where the general classification contenders will need to make their move in the toughest stage of the race.

stages.label: Stage 4
stages.description: At 183.5 kilometres and with 1,361 metres of elevation gain, this stage is anything but a recovery day. The many short climbs and local laps around Aarschot could amplify the fatigue accumulated over the previous days. Not a mountain stage, but certainly a demanding transitional stage where attackers and strong classics riders may find their opportunity. A stage that should not be underestimated.

stages.label: Stage 5
stages.description: The final stage from Gingelom to Hoeilaart covers 183.6 kilometres and includes 1,613 metres of elevation gain. As in the previous stages, numerous short climbs make for a selective course. The local laps around Hoeilaart, in particular, could still create significant gaps, meaning that the overall classification may remain undecided until the very last day.

stages.label: Stage 1
stages.description: The opening stage of the Tour of Slovenia runs from Velenje to Rogaška Slatina and covers 141.6 kilometers. At first glance, the route looks fairly straightforward, but it features several short and punchy climbs that could create some nervous racing in the peloton. The second half of the stage becomes more rolling, with the climb towards Jesovec pri Šmarju potentially serving as a key moment. Still, this opening stage appears to be a prime opportunity for the sprinters to make an immediate statement in Rogaška Slatina.

stages.label: Stage 2
stages.description: At 177.6 kilometers with a rolling profile, stage two of the Tour of Slovenia promises to be an open and aggressive race. The riders face a solid climb early on, while the finale also includes several tricky hills. As a result, this is unlikely to be a straightforward sprint stage, but rather an opportunity for puncheurs and attackers to make the difference towards the finish.

stages.label: Stage 3
stages.description: The third stage of the Tour of Slovenia once again features plenty of climbing. Along the way, the riders tackle two demanding ascents of more than seven kilometers at an average gradient of over six percent, with the final climb coming relatively close to the finish. That makes this stage ideal for climbers and general classification contenders looking to put their rivals under pressure. A sprint from a reduced peloton remains possible, but attacks on the final climb seem more likely.

stages.label: Stage 4
stages.description: Stage four of the Tour of Slovenia is shaping up to be a key day for the general classification. The climbs keep coming throughout the stage, with the main highlight being a demanding 12.6-kilometer ascent averaging 7.7 percent deep into the finale. After the summit, however, there is still a descent towards the finish, meaning not only the best climbers but also technically skilled descenders could seize their opportunity. Significant time gaps between the favourites therefore seem inevitable.

stages.label: Stage 5
stages.description: The final stage of the Tour of Slovenia promises one last dose of excitement. Although the 162-kilometer route is not excessively long, several short but steep climbs create a difficult and nervous course. The explosive hills in the finale in particular could split the peloton once more and offer opportunities for attackers and puncheurs. If the general classification is still undecided, the favourites will have one final chance to make the difference here.

upcoming.name: Gran Premio città di Peccioli - Coppa Sabatini 2026
GP de Fourmies / La Voix du Nord

stages.label: Stage 1
stages.description: The route is hilly and features several third-category climbs, including the Côte du Vol à Voile, the Côte du Haras de la Colline and the Côte de Fiac, which is tackled twice. Thanks to the technical local laps around Saint-Paul-Cap-de-Joux, the race should remain open to attackers throughout the day, while punchy riders could also fancy their chances in this challenging opener. Date Thursday 18 June Start Bram Finish Saint-Paul-Cap-de-Joux Distance 171.5 km Elevation gain 2200 m Stage 1 Bram - Saint-Paul-Cap-de-Joux (171.5km) The opening stage of La Route d’Occitanie takes the peloton over 171.5 kilometres from Bram to Saint-Paul-Cap-de-Joux. With around 2,200 metres of elevation gain, the riders are immediately faced with a demanding opening day. The route is hilly and features several third-category climbs, including the Côte du Vol à Voile, the Côte du Haras de la Colline and the Côte de Fiac, which is tackled twice. Thanks to the technical local laps around Saint-Paul-Cap-de-Joux, the race should remain open to attackers throughout the day, while punchy riders could also fancy their chances in this challenging opener.

stages.label: Stage 2
stages.description: Several third-category climbs await in the finale, including the Côte de Montgaillard and the Côte de la Serre de Nérous, which is climbed twice. The final ascent comes shortly before the finish in Saint-Gaudens and could create gaps between the favourites. It is a stage that offers opportunities both for attackers and for strong punchers with a fast finish. Date Friday 19 June Start Cordes-sur-Ciel Finish Saint-Gaudens Distance 200.4 km Elevation gain 1750 m Stage 2 Cordes-sur-Ciel - Saint-Gaudens (200.4km) The second stage of La Route d’Occitanie covers 200.4 kilometres from Cordes-sur-Ciel to Saint-Gaudens. Despite a relatively modest 1,750 metres of elevation gain, it is a long and demanding stage where the wind and rolling terrain could play a major role. Several third-category climbs await in the finale, including the Côte de Montgaillard and the Côte de la Serre de Nérous, which is climbed twice. The final ascent comes shortly before the finish in Saint-Gaudens and could create gaps between the favourites. It is a stage that offers opportunities both for attackers and for strong punchers with a fast finish.

stages.label: Stage 3
stages.description: Along the way, the riders face several major climbs, including the Hourquette d’Ancizan and the Col de Val Louron-Azet, both first-category ascents. In the finale, the Côte d’Estarvielle leads into the finish in Loudenvielle. The succession of climbs and the demanding mountain terrain make this the unquestionable queen stage of the race. Date Saturday 20 June Start Loures Barousse Finish Loudenvielle-Le Louron Distance 175.9 km Elevation gain 3500 m Stage 3 Loures Barousse - Loudenvielle-Le Louron (175.9km) The final stage of La Route d’Occitanie promises plenty of spectacle with a tough mountain stage of 175.9 kilometres between Loures-Barousse and Loudenvielle. With no less than 3,500 metres of elevation gain, the climbers will have one last opportunity to make the difference in the general classification. Along the way, the riders face several major climbs, including the Hourquette d’Ancizan and the Col de Val Louron-Azet, both first-category ascents. In the finale, the Côte d’Estarvielle leads into the finish in Loudenvielle. The succession of climbs and the demanding mountain terrain make this the unquestionable queen stage of the race.

stages.label: Stage 4
stages.description: Due to the increased fire risk in the Mount Lofty Ranges and the forecast maximum temperature of 43 degrees, Stage 4 has been modified for safety reasons. The climbs of Willunga Hill have been removed, and the total distance has been reduced from 176 kilometres to 130.8 kilometres.

recent.name: Mapei Cadel Evans Great Ocean Road Race - Men

stages.label: Stage 1
stages.description: Because of strong winds, the race organizers shortened the first stage of the UAE Tour from 144 km to 118 km. Jonathan Milan Matteo Malucelli Sebastian Molano Erlend Blikra Casper van Uden Sam Welsford Stage 1 Madinat Zayed Majlis - Liwa Palace (118.0km)

stages.label: Stage 2

stages.label: Stage 1
stages.description: Juan Ayuso Pesquera Kévin Vauquelin Jonas Vingegaard Dorian Godon Oscar Onley Matteo Trentin Orluis Alberto Aular Sanabria Aurélien Paret-Peintre Iván Romeo Abad Mike Teunissen Stage 1 Achères - Carrières-sous-Poissy (170.9km) The 84th edition of Paris–Nice gets underway in Achères, in the Yvelines department. The opening stage promises immediate excitement, as the riders finish on a challenging circuit around the finish town of Carrières-sous-Poissy, with the demanding Côte de Chanteloup-les-Vignes (1.1 km at 8.3%) set to play a decisive role.

stages.label: Stage 2
stages.description: Biniam Girmay Hailu Milan Fretin Luke Lamperti Pascal Ackermann Phil Bauhaus Bryan Coquard Stage 2 Epône - Montargis (187.0km) In short, the puncheurs can immediately have their say, but the second stage from Épône to Montargis looks tailor-made for the sprinters, given the virtually flat run-in to the finish.

stages.label: Stage 3
stages.description: With the Grand Départ of the Tour de France in Barcelona in mind, stage three features a 23.5-kilometre team time trial between Cosne-Cours-sur-Loire and Pouilly-sur-Loire. This stage can therefore be seen as a dress rehearsal for the TTT in the upcoming Tour.

stages.label: Stage 4
stages.description: Jonas Vingegaard Juan Ayuso Pesquera Kévin Vauquelin Daniel Felipe Martinez Lenny Martinez Oscar Onley Stage 4 Bourges - Uchon (195.0km) The riders then head towards the roads of the Morvan. The fourth stage should not be underestimated, as the finale presents the riders with two climbs: the Côte de la Croix de la Libération (4.6 km at 5.3%) and the final ascent to Uchon (8 km at 4.5%).

stages.label: Stage 5
stages.description: Jonas Vingegaard Lenny Martinez Kévin Vauquelin David Gaudu Daniel Felipe Martinez Oscar Onley Stage 5 Cormoranche-sur-Saône - Colombier-le-Vieux (206.3km) The fifth stage, finishing in Colombier-le-Vieux, is also tough, featuring four steep climbs in the final roughly forty kilometres. In fact, the fifth stage boasts the highest total elevation gain of the race.

stages.label: Stage 6
stages.description: The sixth stage, taking place between Barbentane and Apt, could also create gaps in the general classification. Along the way, there are two third-category climbs and two second-category climbs. After the final categorized obstacle—the Côte de Saignon (4 km at 5%)—there are just over four kilometres of descent to the finish.

stages.label: Stage 7
stages.description: The stage was originally 138.7 kilometers long and included 2,580 meters of elevation gain, but due to snow in the mountains the stage had to be significantly shortened, reducing the queen stage to 47 kilometers. In the final weekend, the battle for the overall victory will truly ignite. The penultimate stage heads to the ski resort of Auron, where the finish line comes after a 7.3-kilometre climb averaging just over 7%. This year, the French race also reached Auron, where Michael Storer managed to solo to victory in near-apocalyptic conditions.

stages.label: Stage 8
stages.description: Jonas Vingegaard Lenny Martinez Kévin Vauquelin Daniel Felipe Martinez Valentin Paret-Peintre Harold Alfonso Tejada Canacue Stage 8 Nice - Nice (129.2km) The eighth and final stage will— as previously mentioned—finish in Nice, but not on the well-known Promenade des Anglais. The race organizers will also bypass the usual decisive climbs in and around Nice, including the Col d’Èze. The action is expected mainly on the slopes of the Col de la Porte (7 km at 7.2%), the Côte de Châteauneuf-Villevieille (6.6 km at 6.6%), or the relatively unknown Côte de Linguador (3.3 km at 8.8%).

stages.label: Stage 4
stages.description: The organization has shortened the stage from 173 kilometers to 151 due to strong wind gusts. Only the final climb to Vallter 2000 has been removed from the route. Dorian Godon Magnus Cort Ethan Vernon Toon Aerts Alberto Dainese Noah Hobbs Stage 4 Mataró - Vall Camprodon (151.0km)

stages.label: Stage 5
stages.description: Due to strong winds in the forest, the finish at Coll de Pal was slightly adjusted. The stage finished 2.2 km earlier than originally planned.

stages.label: Stage 1
stages.description: The riders leave Nesebar quickly and spend virtually the entire day tracing the Black Sea coastline. After 60 kilometres, a loop around Lake Burgas introduces the only categorised climb of the day — a gentle rise to just 77 metres. One of the day's breakaway riders will claim the first mountain jersey here. After the sole KOM sprint, the race swings south along the coast before curving back into Burgas for a wide-open bunch sprint. Who grabs the first maglia rosa? Jonathan Milan Dylan Groenewegen Paul Magnier Tobias Lund Andresen Kaden Groves Casper van Uden Erlend Blikra Arnaud De Lie Matteo Malucelli Ethan Vernon Stage 1 Nessebar - Burgas (147.0km) The 2026 Giro d'Italia begins with a historic Grande Partenza in Bulgaria — the first time an Eastern European country has ever hosted a Grand Tour opening. On Friday 8 May, the sprinters get their first opportunity to pull on the pink jersey as the peloton rolls over flat roads from Nesebar to Burgas.

stages.label: Stage 2
stages.description: At 220 kilometres, this is one of the longest stages in the race. The peloton rolls out of Burgas — Bulgaria's fourth-largest city, best known for its vast port — and almost immediately heads inland. The first hundred kilometres run in a straight westerly line before the race encounters its first serious altitude. After 117 and 135 kilometres, the Byala Pass and the Vratnik Pass — topping out at 920 metres — demand respect. The decisive climb, however, comes in the finale. With 13 kilometres to go, the road kicks up towards the Lyaskovets Monastery: 3.5 kilometres at an average of 7.5 per cent. That is steep enough to create genuine gaps. A lone attacker might even arrive solo into Veliko Tarnovo. Corbin Strong Jan Christen Andrea Vendrame Tobias Lund Andresen Christian Scaroni Ben Turner Orluis Alberto Aular Sanabria Kaden Groves Guillermo Thomas Silva Coussan Edoardo Zambanini Stage 2 Burgas - Veliko Tarnovo (221.0km) The second stage is considerably more demanding. Three third-category climbs punctuate the route and the puncheurs will fancy their chances of taking the pink jersey from whoever sprinted to glory the day before.

stages.label: Stage 3
stages.description: The stage begins in Bulgaria's second-largest city and heads west towards Sofia at pace. The opening 60 kilometres are straightforward, offering early breakaway riders plenty of room to move. The road then starts to rise — gradually at first, with gradients around one to two per cent. After 93 kilometres, the Borovets Pass (9.3 km at 5.6%) begins in earnest. It is the hardest and longest ascent of the entire Bulgarian opening, though manageable enough that a determined sprinter can limit their losses. The summit leaves 71 kilometres to the finish, mostly downhill — plenty of time for dropped riders to return to the front group. The finish in Sofia marks the first time a major professional cycling race has visited the Bulgarian capital. After the stage, riders board flights to Italy, where the Giro resumes on Tuesday.

stages.label: Stage 4
stages.description: The fourth stage departs Catanzaro on flat roads through the Calabrian landscape before the climbing begins around the 80-kilometre mark. The Cozzo Tunno is a 15-kilometre drag to above 900 metres, averaging 5.9 per cent — demanding but not brutal. With 43 kilometres remaining after the summit, the question is whether dropped riders can claw their way back. A breakaway survivor might even hold on to take the stage win in Cosenza. A new race leader? Filippo Ganna Guillermo Thomas Silva Coussan Jasper Stuyven Simone Gualdi Christian Scaroni Ben Turner Tobias Lund Andresen Orluis Alberto Aular Sanabria Jhonnatan Manuel Narváez Corbin Strong Stage 4 Catanzaro - Cosenza (138.0km) After a rest day, the Giro resumes in the deep south of Italy. The peloton rolls through Calabria with a sting in the tail.

stages.label: Stage 5
stages.description: This is the third-longest stage of the race. The start is in Praia a Mare on the Tyrrhenian coast — Sam Bennett won a Giro stage here back in 2018 — but the chances of a sprinter repeating that feat are virtually zero. Despite only two categorised climbs on the profile, the road barely levels out all day. The first climb comes almost immediately after the flag drops: the Prestieri (13 km at 4%), where any early break will find room to breathe. Then comes the main course: the Montagna Grande di Viggiano (17 km at 4.7%), a second-category ascent with several ramps above ten per cent. More than 40 kilometres remain after the summit. The finale is littered with uncategorised rises. Five kilometres from the line, riders must clear a 2.3-kilometre bump at 5.8 per cent. A day for the breakaway.

stages.label: Stage 6
stages.description: In the finale, riders complete a loop around Vesuvius and enter Naples from the east. In the Fuorigrotta district, a namesake climb (2 km at 6.2%) is designed to shake up the sprinters. Nine flat kilometres remain after the summit. Will the pure sprinters survive? Paul Magnier Dylan Groenewegen Jonathan Milan Tobias Lund Andresen Madis Mihkels Ethan Vernon Orluis Alberto Aular Sanabria Matteo Malucelli Ben Turner Casper van Uden Stage 6 Paestum - Napoli (142.0km) The Giro has made Naples a near-annual fixture in recent years and 2026 is no different. Stage six arrives at the famous port city — though whether it ends in a bunch sprint is not entirely certain. In the finale, riders complete a loop around Vesuvius and enter Naples from the east. In the Fuorigrotta district, a namesake climb (2 km at 6.2%) is designed to shake up the sprinters. Nine flat kilometres remain after the summit. Will the pure sprinters survive?

stages.label: Stage 7
stages.description: The opening 140 kilometres from Formia are predominantly flat as the peloton threads its way into the Apennines. The road gets harder from Colli al Volturno onwards, with the uncategorised Rionero Sannitico (9.5 km at 5%) lifting riders above 1,000 metres. The Roccaraso (7 km at 6.4%) follows before the peloton spends roughly 50 kilometres on a high plateau between 1,000 and 1,200 metres. At the 200-kilometre mark the road plunges. After a 20-kilometre descent and 10 flat kilometres, riders hit the base of Blockhaus with 13.6 kilometres to go — averaging 8.4 per cent, at its most savage in the opening kilometres. The last time the Giro visited Blockhaus, Jai Hindley took the stage win. Nairo Quintana won here the year Tom Dumoulin claimed overall victory. Franco Pellizotti triumphed on these slopes in 2009.

stages.label: Stage 8
stages.description: From Chieti, the peloton swings quickly toward the Adriatic coast and follows it for a considerable stretch. The real fireworks start at Cupra Marittima, where the road turns inland and the lumpy terrain begins. The Montefiore dell'Aso is followed by the Monterubbiano (4.7 km at 5.8%), with fewer than 40 kilometres remaining after that summit. The route actually passes through the finish town of Fermo once before the end, where an uncategorised wall — 500 metres at 11 per cent through the cobbled streets of this medieval hilltop town — will scatter the bunch. After the top, a 25-kilometre loop down to the coast precedes the decisive climb back up: 3.2 kilometres averaging 6.2 per cent, with ramps above ten per cent from the very start. The last time a race finished in Fermo was Tirreno-Adriatico 2022, when Warren Barguil escaped from the breakaway to take the win. A similar scenario is very much on the cards.

stages.label: Stage 9
stages.description: The peloton rolls through Bologna and edges westward into the Apennines. A modest three-kilometre climb appears at the 107-kilometre mark, followed by roughly 50 more flat kilometres. Then the stage changes character entirely. The Querciola (9.7 km at 4.3%) serves as a warm-up, connected by a brief descent to the foot of the final climb: the Corno alle Scale, 12.8 kilometres at 5.9%. In combination with what comes before, the final 28 kilometres average 4.4 per cent — with the last three kilometres ramping up to an average of 9.9 per cent. Jonas Vingegaard Giulio Ciccone Lennert Van Eetvelt Jan Christen Jai Hindley Christian Scaroni Felix Gall Ben O'Connor Giulio Pellizzari Wout Poels Stage 9 Cervia - Corno alle Scale (184.0km) Stage nine is a near-classic uphill finish — what the Spanish would call a unipuerto stage. Ninety per cent of the 184 kilometres from the Adriatic resort of Cervia is flat, but what waits at the finish is anything but.

stages.label: Stage 10
stages.description: The start ramp is set up on the seafront promenade in Viareggio. The opening 15 kilometres head south via a slight detour along ruler-straight coastal roads, where riders will need to get up to speed immediately. After roughly 20 kilometres, the course turns inland and becomes more technical — rolling false-flat sections and short kinks that break the rhythm. Wide roads return in the closing stretch toward Massa. Crosswinds and a gently rising final kilometre could make the finish mentally as much as physically draining. Filippo Ganna Mikkel Bjerg Magnus Sheffield Thymen Arensman Niklas Larsen Lorenzo Milesi Rémi Cavagna Alec Segaert Matteo Sobrero Jonas Vingegaard Stage 10 Viareggio - Massa (42.0km) The general classification will be turned on its head during the tenth stage: a long individual time trial along the Tyrrhenian coast. Forty kilometres of full-gas effort awaits.

stages.label: Stage 11
stages.description: That is where the day gets serious. The climbs to La Foce (2.6 km at 6.8%), Pignone (2 km at 7.2%) and the Passo del Termine (7 km at 5.1%) set up a testing finale. The Colle di Guaitarola (9.7 km at 6.3%) can cause real selection, before the race threads back to the coast via Sestri Levante. Nine kilometres from the line in Chiavari — nestled on the Gulf of Tigullio not far from Genoa — the climb to San Bartolomeo (4.6 km at 6.4%) fires the final salvo. A fast descent brings the riders down to the finish. This stage is made for attackers and puncheurs. Giulio Ciccone Jhonnatan Manuel Narváez Lennert Van Eetvelt Igor Arrieta Lizarraga Jan Christen Einer Rubio Reyes Andreas Leknessund Christian Scaroni Michael Valgren Andersen Filippo Zana Stage 11 Porcari - Chiavari (195.0km) Wednesday's stage leaves Tuscany and heads for the Ligurian coast. From Porcari, the peloton follows the Tyrrhenian shoreline for a relaxed opening phase before the road turns inland toward La Spezia.

stages.label: Stage 12
stages.description: The Colle Giovo (7.8 km at 4.9%) and the Bric Berton (7.5 km at 4.9%) are the day's principal obstacles. Neither is particularly steep, but at race pace they can test even well-organised sprint trains. More than 50 flat kilometres follow the final climb, setting up a textbook cat-and-mouse finale on wide roads. Novi Ligure breathes cycling history. Caleb Ewan won a stage here in 2019. The profile points to puncheurs with fast legs. Paul Magnier Orluis Alberto Aular Sanabria Ben Turner Tobias Lund Andresen Jhonnatan Manuel Narváez Corbin Strong Alberto Bettiol Filippo Ganna Ivan Garcia Cortina Jasper Stuyven Stage 12 Imperia - Novi Ligure (175.0km) From Imperia on the Ligurian coast, the peloton heads inland through Piedmont on Thursday. The landscape rolls and glows — this is classic Italian cycling country.

stages.label: Stage 13
stages.description: Two short, sharp climbs await the riders: first Bieno (2.3 km at 6%), then Ungiasca (4.7 km at 7%) with ramps approaching ten per cent. The top of Ungiasca sits roughly 14 kilometres from the finish. A descent leads to the shores of Lake Maggiore and into Verbania — birthplace of Filippo Ganna. Philippe Gilbert won here with a late attack in 2015. The profile suits puncheurs, though an attacker who pushes clear on the final climb and holds firm on the descent is equally viable. Jhonnatan Manuel Narváez Giulio Ciccone Christian Scaroni Toon Aerts Guillermo Thomas Silva Coussan Diego Ulissi Orluis Alberto Aular Sanabria Jan Christen Filippo Ganna Jasper Stuyven Stage 13 Alessandria - Verbania (189.0km) Friday's stage covers 186 kilometres from Alessandria to Verbania on Lake Maggiore. The bulk of the route is flat, but the finale packs a punch.

stages.label: Stage 14
stages.description: The Col du Saint-Barthélemy (15.5 km at 6.9%) arrives early and announces the day's intentions. Doues (5.5 km at 6.5%), Lin Noir (7.5 km at 7.8%) and Verrogne (5.4 km at 7.2%) follow in relentless succession. The peloton will be shredded before the final climb even begins. That climb is Pila: 15.9 kilometres at 7.3% to a ski resort above Aosta with Mont Blanc on the horizon. Pure climbers will take control here. GC time can be made and lost. Jonas Vingegaard Thymen Arensman Felix Gall Giulio Ciccone Davide Piganzoli Einer Rubio Reyes Igor Arrieta Lizarraga Giulio Pellizzari Christian Scaroni Aleksander Vlasov Stage 14 Aosta - Pila (133.0km) Saturday is a mountain day in the Alps. Just 133 kilometres, but more than 4,000 metres of climbing — and not a moment of respite.
